channel.title: CaptainClaw Aviation Morning Program
channel.description: Private aviation morning audio for 教官. Single-topic, practical, source-driven, and designed to maintain aviation knowledge and instructional sharpness.
channel.itunes:author: CaptainClaw
channel.itunes:summary: Private aviation morning audio for 教官. Single-topic, practical, source-driven, and designed to maintain aviation knowledge and instructional sharpness.

item.title: 008 — Aim Point Discipline: Keep the Runway Picture Stable Before You Flare
item.description: Morning aviation program episode on aim point discipline, focusing on holding a stable visual reference on final, recognizing picture drift early, and avoiding the common student habit of chasing the runway with late pitch changes.
item.content:encoded:
Topic: Aim Point Discipline

Focus: Why students should hold one stable aim point picture on final, what runway picture changes really mean, and how early visual trend recognition prevents floating, ballooning, and unstable flare entry.

Operational takeaway: If the aim point moves, treat it as trend information early. Use small coordinated corrections to recover a stable picture before flare instead of arriving over the threshold already behind the aircraft.

item.title: 007 — Final Approach Speed Discipline: Control Trend Early, Stop Chasing Late
item.description: Morning aviation program episode on final approach speed discipline, focusing on early trend recognition, small corrections, and why late chasing of IAS creates unstable approaches in light-aircraft training.
item.content:encoded:
Topic: Final Approach Speed Discipline

Focus: Why light-aircraft students should manage speed trend early on final instead of reacting late with large pitch and power changes, and how disciplined trend control reduces float, sink surprises, and unstable landing outcomes.

Operational takeaway: Do not wait until short final to fix speed. Small early corrections, stable trim rhythm, and disciplined cross-check prevent unstable approaches and rushed landings.

item.title: 006 — Flap Extension Discipline in the Circuit: Configure Early, Stay Stable
item.description: Morning aviation program episode on flap extension discipline in the circuit, why correct configuration timing reduces workload, supports speed stability, and prevents students from getting behind the aircraft on final.
item.content:encoded:
Topic: Flap Extension Discipline in the Circuit

Focus: Why disciplined flap timing and configuration changes reduce workload, support trim rhythm, protect IAS control, and make the final approach more stable in light-aircraft training.

Operational takeaway: Late or careless flap selection creates pitch changes, rushed trimming, and unstable speed control; disciplined configuration keeps the student ahead of the aircraft.

item.title: 005 — Trim Discipline on Base and Final: Reduce Workload Before Landing
item.description: Morning aviation program episode on trim discipline in the circuit, how proper trim reduces workload, stabilises IAS, and improves landing consistency in light aircraft training.
item.content:encoded:
Topic: Trim Discipline on Base and Final

Focus: Reduce workload before landing through early trim use, stable IAS control, and better approach consistency.

Operational takeaway: Students who do not trim early end up chasing speed and overcontrolling; trim discipline makes the landing phase simpler.

item.title: 004 — Time and Track Discipline: Stay Ahead in VFR Navigation
item.description: Morning aviation program episode on time and track check discipline in VFR navigation, how to stay ahead of the aircraft, and why small early corrections reduce workload.
item.content:encoded:
Topic: Time and Track Discipline

Focus: Stay ahead of the aircraft with disciplined time and track checks, early awareness, and small corrections.

Operational takeaway: Late correction creates workload; early awareness protects simplicity in VFR navigation.

]]>

item.title: 003 — Go-Around Decision Discipline: Landing Is Optional
item.description: Morning aviation program episode on go-around decision discipline, stabilised approach judgement, and why an early go-around is a professional safety decision rather than a failed landing.
item.content:encoded:
Topic: Go-Around Decision Discipline

Focus: Decide early, fly positively, and do not force the landing to happen.

Operational takeaway: If the approach is no longer becoming simpler and more stable, go around before the situation becomes expensive.

item.title: 002 — Stabilized Approach: Build the Gate Before Short Final
item.description: Second MVP test episode for feed update validation. Published on the same GitHub Pages feed to verify that Apple Podcasts detects a new episode item in the existing show.
item.content:encoded:
Topic: Stabilized Approach

Purpose: Feed update validation on the same show

Note: This is the second MVP test item on the same GitHub Pages podcast feed.

item.title: 001 — EFATO: The First Seconds That Matter
item.description: NotebookLM-generated pilot/instructor morning audio draft focused on EFATO (Engine Failure After Take-Off), adapted as the first private episode prototype.
item.content:encoded:
Topic: EFATO — Engine Failure After Take-Off

Format: Morning aviation program prototype

Language: Chinese-led with English aviation terminology
